generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the ink smudged from the cold and the tears that won't cease to fall. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, and I can feel the sea pressing in like a living thing, demanding to be heard. I've been here for thirty years, weathering storms that would have swallowed lesser men whole, but tonight feels different. Tonight, I fear I may not make it through.

You were never meant to be a child of the sea, my Eleanor. You were meant to be a child of the sun, of gardens and laughter, of the kind of life that doesn't involve storms and salt and the endless, relentless dark. But you chose your own path, and I... I chose to let you go. I told myself it was for your own good. That I was protecting you from the cruel, unrelenting world I knew. But I fear I was only protecting myself from the truth—that I was not the father you needed, and that I had no right to hold you back.

I've been thinking about you a lot these past few days, as the storm clouds gather and the sea grows restless. I've found myself staring at the old photographs, the ones of you as a child, with your mother's eyes and my stubborn chin, and I remember the days when you would run through the lighthouse with your little hands full of seaweed and seashells

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with a fury that seems to mirror the tempest within my own heart.** Five years have passed since our last conversation, and I find myself at a loss for words—yet the storm has forced me to speak, to reach out to you, my dearest daughter, in a way I never could before.

**You were always the light in my life, Eleanor, the one who brought warmth to these cold, lonely nights.** I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through the stone walls of this lighthouse, filling it with life. Now, the silence is deafening, and the only sound is the relentless crash of the waves against the rocks below.

**I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if this letter will ever reach you.** But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel a strange compulsion to write. Perhaps it is the sea itself, whispering to me, reminding me of the promise I once made to protect you, to guide you, to be a beacon in your life as you were in mine.

**I have always believed in the power of light, both literal and metaphorical.** The lighthouse stands as a symbol of hope, a guiding light for those who sail the treacherous waters. In the same way, I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may find you in a state of disbelief. I write to you now, on this storm-laden night, with a heart heavy with years of silence and regret. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it were crying out in mourning for something lost. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but I feel it is my duty to speak these words to you, even if they are but echoes in the void between us.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in all that time. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. It is as though you were never here at all. I have tried to move on, to find solace in the routine of this lighthouse, but the nights are long, and the silence of your absence has grown louder with each passing year.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who had the world at her feet, and yet she chose to walk away. I do not know what drove you to it, nor do I wish to know. I have no right to question you, for I was blind to the pain you must have felt. I was a father who failed to see the storm coming, and by the time I did, it was too late.

I have watched

**[The letter is written in a weathered, hand-stitched envelope, the ink smudged from the damp sea air. The paper is yellowed and crinkled, as if it has been folded and unfolded many times. The words are penned in a rough, urgent hand, with some lines crossed out and rewritten, as though the writer struggled to find the right words.]**

---

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast in my ears, the sea raging against the cliffs as though it would devour the very rocks beneath me. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I’ve spent five years trying not to think of you, trying to forget the day you left—how you stood at the door, your eyes like ice, and said you would never return.

But now, as the sky turns the color of blood and the waves grow louder, I find myself thinking of you more than I should. I’ve kept the lantern lit all night, as I always do, but tonight, it feels like the light is dimmer, as if it’s trying to reach you, to find you, to bring you back.

I’ve had no news of you in all this time. I’ve heard nothing but the wind and the sea. I’ve tried to believe that you’re safe, that you’ve found your own path, but I can’t shake the feeling that I’ve

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not reach you in time. The winds have been howling all night, and the sea, which has long been my constant companion, roars with a fury I have not seen in many a year. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the hope that if I do not survive this night, my words may serve as a final plea for understanding.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have tried to fill the silence with the work of the lighthouse, it has not been enough. The light, which I have kept burning for all these years, has not been enough to light the way between us. I have watched the dawn rise each morning, and I have seen the sun set each evening, but none of it has brought you back to me.

I know that the world has moved on since you left. You have found your own path, and I have not been there to guide you. I have heard the stories, the rumors, the whispers of what you have become. I have not sought them out, for I would rather believe that you are well and happy, even if I cannot be sure. But I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you, and I have not stopped loving you.

The storm is upon us now. The waves are rising, and the wind is fierce.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with longing, for I know you will not read these words until it is too late. Yet, I must say them, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and the silence has grown colder than the winds that howl through the cliffs.

You were young when you left, Eleanor, and I did not understand. I saw only your stubbornness, your refusal to listen to reason. You were a flame, and I feared you would burn yourself out in the dark. But I was wrong, my child. You were not a flame; you were a star, and I, a fool, feared the light.

These past years have been a torment, not of absence, but of regret. I have watched the sea from this tower, night after night, and in its endless rhythm, I have heard your voice. I have seen the moonlight on the waves, and I have thought of you. I have prayed for you, and I have mourned the distance between us.

Tonight, a storm is coming—great and terrible, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with fury. I have no doubt that the light will be needed, and I will stand guard, as I have always done. But I fear for my own life, for the sea has been cruel to me, and I am not as young

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has long since reconciled with the past, but with the weight of a soul that has carried its burdens in silence for far too long. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have tried to find solace in the quiet of this lighthouse, the storm that now looms on the horizon has stirred something deep within me—something I have not dared to name.

The wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea, once a familiar companion, now roars with a fury I have never known. I fear for my life, and in that fear, I am reminded of the storms of my heart—those moments when I could not bear to speak, when the words I longed to say were swallowed by shame, by pride, by the silence that grew between us.

You were once my light, my greatest hope, and I have let that light fade. I have let the years pass without a word, without a letter, without a single attempt to reach out. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. And yet, I still feel the pull of your name, the echo of your laughter, the memory of your eyes—bright, fierce, and full of life.

I have spent these years watching the sea, not for its beauty, but for its warning. I have watched the waves

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I hope you've found peace, or at least some semblance of it. I hope you've learned to forgive yourself for the things you did and didn't do. I hope you've found someone to love, and that they love you in return. I hope you've never had to face a storm like this alone.

But I'm writing this because I can't bear the thought of you hearing about my death from some stranger's lips. I'm writing this because I want to say the things I never could before. I'm writing this because I'm afraid I won't be here when the storm breaks.

You were five when you first came to the lighthouse. A small, wide-eyed girl with a ribbon in your hair and a look in your eyes that made me think of the sea. I remember the way you'd sit on the steps, watching the waves, and how you'd ask me if the sea ever cried. I told you it did, but only when the moon was full and the wind was right. I told you the sea cried in the way it whispered secrets to the shore, and how the waves would crash like a mother's sigh.

I remember the day you left. It was a cold spring morning, and you stood at the door with a suitcase and a suitcase full of silence. I tried to say something, but my voice failed me. I remember the way you looked at me,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, by the dim light of the oil lamp, with a heart heavy with years of silence and a mind troubled by the howling wind that threatens to tear the very roof of this old keep from its moorings. The storm is coming, and though the sea has always been my constant companion, I fear this night may be the last.

Five long years have passed since you left this place, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your hand, nor heard your voice. You were but a child when you left, and now I wonder if time has not only aged me but has also turned you into something I cannot reach. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried, but the letters I sent—so many of them—were met with silence, or worse, with the cold indifference of a daughter who has chosen the world over her father.

I do not blame you, perhaps. You were young, and the world is a cruel and vast thing. But I have always believed that blood is thicker than water, and that no storm, no matter how fierce, can sever the bond between parent and child. Yet I fear that the sea may be more forgiving than I am.

Tonight, the sky is dark as a sailor's soul, and the wind howls like a banshee. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1897**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside this old stone tower. The sea, that relentless, unfeeling thing, roars with a fury I have never known. I fear the night may take me before the dawn. And yet, I must write, for I have no one else to speak to, no one else to confess the truths I have kept locked away for five long years.

You were never meant to be the daughter of a lighthouse keeper, Eleanor. You were born for the world beyond these cliffs, for the grandeur of the city, for the society of men who speak in measured tones and wear their hearts in their pockets. I saw it in your eyes, that restless fire, that hunger for more than the tides and the stars. I tried to give you the world, but the world would not take you, and I was not the man to see you go.

I remember the day you left, the way your bonnet fluttered in the wind like a white bird, and the way your mother’s tears fell like rain on the cobblestones. You did not look back, and I did not call you. We both knew the time had come. You had your life, and I had this tower, this endless vigil over the sea.

But now, as the storm gathers, I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years. The sea howls beyond the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the wind has taken the last of the lantern's oil. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, and if I do, I shall not be the same man who wrote this letter. But I must, for you.

Five years have passed since your mother passed, and five years since you left this place, the only home you ever knew. I have not heard from you, not a single word, and I have not dared to write. But tonight, the storm comes with a fury I have never known, and I find myself at the edge of something I cannot name.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. No more than twelve, I believe. I remember the day you stood at the door, your small hand clutching the hem of your dress, your eyes wide with a mixture of fear and defiance. You looked at me as though I were a stranger, and perhaps I was. I had become a man of stone and salt, hardened by the sea and the solitude of this place. I had forgotten how to speak to a child, how to listen to a heart.

But I did not forget the look of your face when you left. It haunts me still. I have not dared to dream of you since, for fear that in my

**Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*October 12th, 1896*  
*To My Dearest Eliza,*  

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as heavy as the storm clouds gathering on the horizon. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I fear this may be the last letter I ever write. But I must try, for I have not yet given up on you, and I have not yet given up on myself.

You were once a bright light in my life, like the beacon I tend each night. You were the one who would have stayed by my side, who would have understood the loneliness of this place, who would have known that even in the darkest night, there is still a way to find the dawn. But you left, Eliza. You left without a word, without a look, without even a goodbye. And I have never dared to ask where you went. I feared you would not answer, and I feared that if I did, I would lose you forever.

I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to the wind, and keeping the light burning. It is a solemn duty, and I have done it with pride. But I have also done it with sorrow. I have not been the man I once was. The years have worn me down, and the silence has taken its toll. I have not spoken of you

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the ink barely clinging to the page as the wind howls like a wounded beast against the lighthouse windows. The sea, that restless thing, has been growling all day, as if it too knows the storm is coming. I’ve been watching the horizon for hours, counting the minutes between the thunderclaps, and I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I’ve tried to forget the day you left, the way your eyes turned cold as the sea in the winter, the way you said, *“You don’t understand, Father.”* I’ve tried to believe you were right, that I did not understand. But now, as I sit here in this crumbling tower, the weight of the world pressing down on my shoulders like the very stone of this place, I find I do understand. I understand the silence, the ache of absence, the way the sea has grown louder in your absence.

You were always my light, my little star. I used to tell you stories of the stars, how they never blinked, how they watched over us all. But now, I wonder if I’ve been blind to the truth. The stars don’t blink, but they do fade. And I fear I have, too.

The storm is here now, Eliza. The wind screams through the cracks in the walls, and the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, not with the same hand that once held your hand as you first stepped into the world, but with the steady, if weathered, hand of a man who has long since grown used to the cold and the solitude of this lighthouse. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have been long, they have not dulled the ache of your absence. Tonight, as the storm gathers on the horizon, I find myself compelled to write, not merely to recall the past, but to speak to you as if you were still here, as if the world had not torn us apart.

The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars with a fury that seems almost human. The waves crash against the cliffs with a force that shakes the very foundations of this tower. I have seen storms before, many of them, and I have weathered them with the same resolve that I have carried through the years of silence. Yet tonight feels different. There is a weight in the air, a foreboding that I cannot shake. I have not told anyone, not even the old sailor who comes to check on the lighthouse, but I fear this storm may be the end of me. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the tempest approaches, I find myself wondering if I will live to see

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that has long since lost its tip, and with hands that tremble not from cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, my child. Five years since you left this house, this life, this *me*. I have not seen you, nor heard from you, nor even dared to write. But tonight—tonight is different. Tonight, the sea roars like a beast in pain, and the wind howls as if it knows the truth of my heart. And I fear that I may not see another dawn.

I have always been a man of quiet strength, of duty, of silence. But you, Eleanor, you were the light in my life. You were the fire that warmed the cold stones of this lighthouse, the voice that softened the thunder of the waves. And now, you are gone. Gone like the tide, and I have been left to stand alone on the shore.

I know I have not been a good father. I know I have been a fool, a man who let pride and duty harden his heart. I should have fought for you, should have stood up to your mother and the world that sought to break us. But I was afraid. Afraid of losing you, afraid of losing myself. And so I let you go. I let you walk away, and I let the silence grow between us, thick as the fog that rolls

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*To my dear, wayward daughter,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea has been restless all day, and now the sky is dark as a funeral shroud, the clouds churning with a fury that speaks of a storm unlike any I've seen in my years here. I've spent most of the evening pacing these halls, the weight of my silence pressing upon me like the tide. And now, as I sit by the window, the light flickering in the storm's grasp, I feel the need to speak, even if it's to a ghost.

Five years have passed since you left, and I've tried to convince myself that I've moved on. But the nights are long, and the loneliness is a cold thing that creeps in through the cracks in the stone. I've seen the sea turn against its own, and I've seen the light fail. I've seen the storm come, and I've seen it go. But tonight, I fear it may not go at all.

I've always known that you were not like the others who came to this place. You were too sharp, too clever, too much like me. I tried to shelter you from the world, but you found your own path. And I, in my foolish pride, let you go. I thought I was

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you this evening with trembling hands, the wind howling like the spirits of the sea, and the waves crashing against the cliffs as though they wish to swallow us whole. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I fear the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I have often wondered if you still remember me, or if the years have faded you from your thoughts as surely as the tide erases the shore.

I am the keeper of this lighthouse, a lonely post where the light must burn through every storm and every calm, no matter the cost. The sea has always been my companion, and I have learned to trust its fury, to read its moods, to know when it is in its wrath or in its grace. But tonight, it is in its wrath. The sky is a bruised purple, the air thick with the scent of salt and impending ruin. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since you left, but I wonder if the light will be enough.

You were always a child of the sea, Eliza, as I was. You spoke of the tides and the stars, of the way the moon pulls the waves like a lover's hand. I remember the day you first stood on the cliffs with your father and me, your small hands gripping the railing as the waves danced below. You said you wished to be a sailor one day,

My Dearest Eleanor,

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that would make even the most seasoned sailor tremble. Tonight, the sky is dark as a storm cloud, and the waves crash against the rocks with a vengeance that seems to echo the turmoil within my heart. I have not seen you in five long years, and I fear that this night may be the last I shall ever see the dawn.

I write this with trembling hands, for I do not know if I shall live to see the morning. The lighthouse is old, and its bones creak like a man with a heavy heart. The storm is upon us, and I feel its presence in every gust of wind that rattles the shutters. I have always been a man of faith, but even I must admit that the gods have been cruel in their silence.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I know that my silence has been a wound upon your soul. I have been a poor father, a distant father, and I cannot undo the years that have passed. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a guardian of the sea, and yet I have failed to be a guardian to you. I have not been there for you when you needed me most. I have not heard your voice, nor felt your touch, and I have not known the joy of your laughter.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have often thought of you, the words have never come easy. But now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself at a crossroads—between duty, memory, and the fragile hope that perhaps, in this moment, I may still reach you.

You were once the light in my life, the daughter I held close to my heart, and I have carried the pain of your absence like a shadow. I have not spoken to you in years, not because I have forgotten you, but because I have feared the silence between us would be too great to break. Yet, as the wind howls and the sea roars, I find myself questioning whether I have been wrong to hold back.

I have always believed that the lighthouse must burn, that the light must shine, even in the darkest of nights. But tonight, I am not certain I will see the dawn. The storm is fierce, and the waves are rising with a fury I have never known. I have spent my life standing in this tower, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight, I feel as though I am being guided by forces far greater than myself.

I do not know if I will survive this night, and if I do, I do not know what I will be left with.

**A Letter to My Daughter**

My dear Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the message you were hoping to receive. Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I find myself sitting here in this lighthouse, writing with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the sea from me.

You were always a bright light, my Clara, but I fear I've cast a shadow over your life. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears and your voice broke as you said, "I can't stay here, Papa." I tried to understand, to reach out, but you turned away. I was too stubborn, too proud, and I let pride silence my love.

I've been alone in this lighthouse, watching the waves crash against the rocks, the stars above, and the silence that has settled between us. I've thought of you every night, wondering if you're safe, if you're happy. I've written letters, but never sent them, afraid of what you might say. Now, as the storm approaches, I feel the weight of my silence, the burden of my choices.

I want you to know that I have never stopped loving you, even when you walked away. I've carried the pain of your absence like a storm in my heart, and I've been waiting for the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that looms on the horizon, and with hands that tremble not only from the chill of the wind but from the weight of the years that have passed since we last spoke. The sea, as ever, is restless, and the night air is thick with the scent of salt and sorrow. I pray this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from the shores of my heart.

Five long years have passed since you left, and though I have not seen you, I have not forgotten you. I have kept your name on my lips in the stillness of the night, and I have whispered your name to the wind as it howls through the cracks of this old lighthouse. You were a light in my life, Eleanor, a light that has since been extinguished, though I still feel its warmth in my memories.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light from this tower. The sea has been rising, and the winds have grown fiercer, as if the very heavens are weeping. I have lit the beacon, as I always do, and I have prayed that it may guide the ships that dare to brave these waters. But I fear the beacon may not be enough to save me, and I fear that I may not live to see the dawn.

I have no regrets, not in the way you might think.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as though it might fracture beneath the weight of what I must say. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and yet tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers—there is no one else to whom I may pour out my soul.

You were but a child when you left, a mere shadow of the girl who once clung to my apron strings with the desperation of a child who knows not the meaning of the word *abandon*. I told you I could not keep you. I told you I was not a father, but a keeper of light, bound to the sea and the sky, not to the fragile, fleeting things of this world. And so you went, with your mother's blessing, with your father's curse, and with the promise that I would not be your father, but merely a man who had once held you.

But I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the way your eyes would widen when you saw the light from the tower, as though it were a thing of magic, a beacon from heaven. I have not forgotten the way you would whisper, *“It’s like a star fallen to earth,”* and I would smile, thinking of the day you would return, a

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you cold. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. I write with trembling hands, for the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that has grown heavy with the weight of unspoken words. I have tried to forget you, as one forgets the ghost of a dream. But the sea has a way of reminding us of what we have lost.

You were but a child when you left, and yet it felt as though a part of me had been taken with you. I have watched the years pass, and though I have kept the lighthouse, I have not kept your memory. I have not kept the way you used to laugh, or the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the horizon. I have not kept the way you would press your small hands to the glass and whisper, *“I want to be a sailor, Papa.”*

I have kept the lighthouse, but not the man I once was. The storms have made me brittle, and the silence has made me cruel. I have become a shadow of the father you once knew,

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Blackmoor Lighthouse, dated October 23rd, 1889]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like the wails of the damned and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. The lantern is lit, the beacon burns bright, and I stand here, alone, with only the salt air and the memory of your laughter to keep me company. I fear I may not live to see dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not live to see you again. But I must write, Eliza, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have no one else to tell my story to.

You were a child when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I thought I was protecting you, that the world was too cruel, too cold, too full of men who would take advantage of a girl with no father and no money. I thought I was keeping you safe. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was wrong to think I knew what was best for you, and I was wrong to let pride and fear keep me from reaching out. You were not a burden, nor were you a mistake. You were my daughter, and I was your father, and I have been a poor one.

I remember the day you left

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I sit here by the great window of this lighthouse, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside. The wind whips against the glass, and the waves crash with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I’ve always said the sea is a fickle mistress, but tonight, it feels as though she’s been waiting for me to finally break. I fear I may not be strong enough to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and I’ve spent every night since then staring at the horizon, hoping to see your silhouette on the shore. I’ve kept the light burning day and night, not just for the ships, but for you. I’ve told myself that if I keep the light alive, you’ll come back. That the beacon will guide you home. But the truth is, I’ve been alone in this tower for so long, I’ve forgotten how to speak to anyone but the sea.

I know you’ve been gone for years, Elara. I know you’ve built a life far from this place. But I still dream of the days we spent together, of your laughter echoing through these halls, of your hands finding mine in the darkness. I can’t help but wonder if you ever thought of me at all.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I’ve seen in my years here. The sky is black as pitch, and the wind is so fierce, it rattles the very bones

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not.** I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in even longer. Time has a way of slipping through one's fingers like sand, and I fear that I have let it slip too far. **A Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*My Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless, and I fear I may not make it through the night. But if I do, I hope you'll read this, and perhaps, just perhaps, you'll understand.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out of our home with a suitcase and a heart full of anger. I remember the way your eyes burned with betrayal, the way your voice cracked as you told me you'd never come back. I didn't believe you then, not really. I thought I'd made a mistake in letting you go.

But I was wrong. You didn't come back because you wanted to. You came back because you had to. I know now, in the silence of this storm, that you were never truly gone. You were just waiting for me to realize what I'd lost.

I've been here alone, watching the waves, counting the hours, and keeping the light burning. You were my world, Eleanor, and I let it all go.
